Effective Listening & Probing: A score of 1 involves a customer typically having to repeat themselves as the agent/user has misunderstood the customers problem. A score of 2 would be achieved if a agent/ user involves understands most common queries and able to support beyond original customer request. A score of 3 shows an agent can effectively ask questions using a funnelled approach in order to discover latent needs. A score of 4 shows an agent is able to coach others on useful questions to ask and advise when a customer message is unclear
Personalisation & Clarity of Response: A score of 1 involves an agent providing answers which feel overly scripted and answers which may not be the most suitable. A score of 2 is if an agent is able to tailor responses to a range of customer needs. If an speakers with short and concise answers. A score of 3 is if an agent speaks with conviction and is able to personalise their tone and language to the customer profile and needs (for example, if a customer is frustrated then an agent should be apologetic to the customers situation). A score of 4 shows a high level of sophistication with short, simple language which is highly tailored to the customers situation and profile
Swift & Punctual Conversations: A score of 1 indicates an agent may the confidence to respond in a prompt manner, for example, they may take, on average, at least 15 seconds to reply to each customer message. Sentences include grammar and punctuation mistakes. In general the conversation feels slow. A score of 2 indicates an agent responds in a timeline manner on average 10 seconds to respond to each message. Sentence structure of agent is clear and legible. A score of 3 able to provide a fast response to common and complex queries which are highly punctual. A score of 4 indicates an agent would be a go-to point of contact for other agents on how to be highly punctual and deliver fast responses to customers
Professional Empathy & Ownership: A score of 1 shows a lack of basic empathy and care when engaging with a customer and is sometimes rude. A score of 2 shows an agent is able to apply empathy statements to a range of scenarios and ownership statements which inform the customer you are keen to own and resolve their problem for them. A score of 3 shows that an agent has excellent understanding of a customers emotional situation especially when dealing with difficult topics such as financial difficulty and the cost of living crisis. A score of 4 shows an agent could coach another agent on empathy and ownership skills
Product and Process Knowledge: A score of 1 would indicate a limited understanding of product issues and unable to explain these issues such as payment errors in detail to customers. A score of 2 shows a good understanding of product issues and shows a good understanding of the 2015 Consumer Rights Act. An agent is also able to differentiate between a dissatisfied customer and when a complaint should be logged. A formal complaint should be logged whenever negligent actions by other staff members or by the company may have potentially taken place such as duplicative payments. A score of 3 shows an agent understands when to apply a ‘good will gesture’ this is when a customer has had a negative experience which should be corrected by compensation such as a 20 pound refund due to cover the inconvenience caused. An agent who scores 3 will understand the importance of this in order to maintain good customer experience. A score of 4 takes place when an agent has covered all past requirements and is able to give detailed explanations of product and process related areas
Conflict Resolution: A score of 1 if the agent struggles to resolve conflicts and handle difficult customers professionally and diplomatically. A score of 2 indicates the agent demonstrates some ability to handle conflicts, but there are occasional lapses in professionalism or effectiveness. A score of 3 involves the agent consistently resolves conflicts and handles difficult customers with professionalism and diplomacy. A score of 4 if the agent goes above and beyond in resolving conflicts, consistently exceeding customer expectations and achieving positive outcomes.
Rapport Building: A score of 1 if the agent has difficulty establishing and maintaining positive relationships with customers. A score of 2 indicates the agent shows some ability to build rapport but may struggle in maintaining it consistently. A score of 3 if the agent consistently establishes and maintains positive relationships with customers through empathy, active listening, and genuine care. A score of 4 if the agent excels in rapport building and consistently exceeds customer expectations, resulting in strong customer loyalty.
Sales Techniques: A score of 1 if the agent lacks basic sales skills and struggles to upsell or handle objections effectively. A score of 2 if the agent demonstrates some ability to use sales techniques, but there is room for improvement in upselling and handling objections. A score of 3 involves the agent consistently uses effective sales techniques such as active selling, upselling, and objection handling. A score of 4 indicates the agent excels in sales techniques, consistently achieving high conversion rates and meeting or exceeding sales targets.
Time Management: A score of 1 if the agent struggles to manage time effectively, resulting in delays or incomplete tasks. A score of 2 indicates the agent demonstrates some ability to manage time effectively but may occasionally face challenges in prioritization or task completion. A score of 3 involves the agent consistently manages time effectively, ensuring all customer interactions are handled promptly without compromising quality. A score of 4 if the agent goes above and beyond in time management, consistently exceeding performance expectations and delivering exceptional results in a timely manner.
